evidence: ExploreResponseBuilder.buildEdges provides a clean, single-method handoff from model objects (ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult) to graph Edge lists — the translation boundary is explicit and lives in one obvious place.
buildExploreResponseBody is a shared private method used by both single-seed and multi-seed handlers, preventing duplication at the response-assembly seam.

buildBaseExploreResponse extracts the common explore response structure — parameters, status, relation_type — into a single factory, reducing the risk of divergence between the two exploration endpoints.
Error and lifecycle propagation at boundaries is consistent: IOException → HTTP 500, IllegalArgumentException → HTTP 400 in all four ExplorationHandlers methods.

The response-building seam is split between ExplorationHandlers (for seed_collocates, discovered_nouns, core_collocates keys) and ExploreResponseBuilder (for edges) — a single response is assembled across two classes in the same package, which is manageable but not self-evident.

handleSemanticFieldComparison builds its 'adjectives' list by calling formatAdjectiveProfile inline rather than sharing the method with ExploreResponseBuilder. The formatAdjectiveProfile helper lives in ExplorationHandlers, not the response builder.

ComparisonResult.getFullyShared(), getPartiallyShared(), and getSpecific() are computed fresh on every call by streaming the full adjective list; handleSemanticFieldComparison calls all three to populate count fields, meaning three full passes over the adjective list at the boundary.
ExplorationHandlers.parseBaseExploreRequest is a private helper that all four exploration handlers call as a first step — clean, eliminates the URI parsing duplication.

The ExploreOptions record bundles single-seed-only parameter nounsPerSeed alongside cross-path parameters, creating a slightly awkward handoff for the multi-seed caller that ignores it (the code defensively rejects nouns_per in multi-seed requests at the HTTP layer).

strengths: ExploreResponseBuilder provides a named, explicit translation seam for model-to-graph conversion — the boundary is one class, one method.
Error handling at all exploration handler boundaries is consistent: IOException→500, IllegalArgumentException→400, with no mixed strategies.
parseBaseExploreRequest shared preamble eliminates URI-parsing duplication across all four handlers.
buildBaseExploreResponse factories the common explore response structure, preventing parameter map divergence between single-seed and multi-seed paths.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are seam-splitting (response assembly across two classes) and minor boundary inefficiency (repeated streaming passes in ComparisonResult), rather than systemic glue-code entropy.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The integration seams are generally clean and predictable: named handoff methods, consistent error propagation, shared preamble helpers. The score is reduced by two real friction points: response assembly is split between ExplorationHandlers (fields) and ExploreResponseBuilder (edges) for the same HTTP response, requiring a reader to look in two places; and ComparisonResult's derived views (getFullyShared etc.) are called three times at the boundary without a caching layer, doing redundant stream passes. Neither issue is severe, but they are the type of 'where does this go?' confusion that costs developer time.

issues.summary: Exploration response assembly is split across ExplorationHandlers (fields) and ExploreResponseBuilder (edges) for the same HTTP response
evidence: buildExploreResponseBody in ExplorationHandlers builds seed_collocates, discovered_nouns, core_collocates, then calls ExploreResponseBuilder.buildEdges for edges — the single response map is populated across two classes.

formatAdjectiveProfile in ExplorationHandlers serialises AdjectiveProfile to map; ExploreResponseBuilder serialises AdjectiveProfile to Edge — two classes each partially understand AdjectiveProfile serialisation.
issues.suggestion: Move all ExplorationResult→response-map logic into ExploreResponseBuilder, making it the sole owner of the exploration-to-HTTP translation seam. ExplorationHandlers then calls ExploreResponseBuilder.buildExploreResponse(result) for the entire body, removing the split.

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldComparison calls getFullyShared/getPartiallyShared/getSpecific producing three full streaming passes over the adjective list at the boundary
evidence: response.put("fully_shared_count", result.getFullyShared().size()); response.put("partially_shared_count", result.getPartiallyShared().size()); response.put("specific_count", result.getSpecific().size()); — three separate stream passes.
Each derived view re-streams the full adjective list with its own filter predicate.
issues.suggestion: Add a ComparisonResult.getSummary() method returning a record with fullySharedCount, partiallySharedCount, and specificCount computed in a single pass, or cache the derived lists in ComparisonResult at construction time since it is immutable. The boundary caller then reads pre-computed values.

root_causes: ExploreResponseBuilder was extracted to own edge-building (model-cycle-fix) but partial response fields stayed in ExplorationHandlers, leaving the split in place.
ComparisonResult derived views were designed as convenient query methods rather than pre-computed summary state.
